summary:
The Red Fort (Lal Qila) of Delhi (construction 1638–1648 AD) was the principal palace complex of the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]] during [[event:2506|Shah Jahan's]] reign and the symbolic seat of Mughal sovereignty for over 200 years — a massive red sandstone fortified palace on the banks of the Yamuna River in Delhi that housed the Peacock Throne (brought from Persia by Shah Jahan and later seized by [[event:2447|Nader Shah]]), the Divan-i-Khas (Hall of Private Audience with its famous inscription 'If there is paradise on earth, it is here, it is here, it is here'), and the apartments of hundreds of imperial family members, officials, and attendants. Shah Jahan had moved his capital from Agra to the newly planned city of Shahjahanabad (Old Delhi), with the Red Fort as its centerpiece.

The Red Fort's significance transcends its Mughal origins. After the British suppressed the [[event:103|Mutiny/Rebellion of 1857]], the last Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah Zafar was tried in the Red Fort and exiled to Burma; the British garrisoned the fort and demolished many of its subsidiary buildings. On August 15, 1947 — Indian Independence Day — Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru raised the Indian flag at the Red Fort's main gate (the Lahori Gate) in the ceremony that ended British rule, establishing a tradition maintained by every subsequent prime minister on Independence Day. The choice of the Red Fort as the site of this ceremony was deliberately symbolic: it connected independent India's birth to the seat of Mughal imperial sovereignty while implicitly asserting continuity with India's pre-colonial past. The Red Fort was designated a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2007.
